action.hypothesis: After nine non-improving rounds and non-competitive K=128 results from topk and routed, the current global support-ranking direction looks structurally weak. The newly added `group_topk` family is the next fundamentally different architecture probe because it imposes local winner-take-all competition before global selection, which may improve feature diversity and reduce redundant support allocation under the same validated training regime.
action.summary: Run the new `group_topk` architecture in proxy at K=128 with the current strongest gated training settings to test whether local competition produces a better quality or cost-quality tradeoff than topk, routed, and gated.
action.self_review: This satisfies the severe-stagnation requirement to try a fundamentally different architecture rather than another minor sweep. The main risk is that the grouping prior is too restrictive at K=128, but it is a cleaner structural change than more tuning on already underperforming families.
notes_to_memory: The topk and routed K=128 probes were both materially worse than the gated anchor, which strengthens the case that plain global support ranking is not the missing ingredient.
This round gives the newly introduced `group_topk` family its first proxy read under the strongest validated training regime so the architecture comparison stays clean.
`group_topk` changes the support-selection inductive bias through local competition rather than another optimizer or learning-rate tweak, which is the right mode under continued stagnation.
next_hypotheses: If `group_topk` proxy FVU is competitive with or better than topk and routed, keep the family alive and stabilize `group_topk_size` as the next family-specific axis.
If `group_topk` is only modest on quality but shows better runtime or memory behavior, evaluate it as a Pareto-tradeoff family rather than discarding it immediately.
If `group_topk` is clearly worse on both quality and cost signals, retire this local-competition idea and move to a more expressive multi-branch or factorized encoder family.
